contents: SYSTEMATIC REVIEWBJDBritish Journal of DermatologyA systematic review and critical evaluation of reportedpathogenic sequence variants in hidradenitis suppurativaJ.W. Frew iD ,1,2,3 D.A. Vekic,1,2,3 J. Woods1,2,3 and G.D. Cains1,2,31Department of Dermatology, Liverpool Hospital, Sydney, New South Wales, Australia2University of New South Wales, Sydney, New South Wales, Australia3Ingham Institute of Applied Medical Research, Liverpool, New South Wales, AustraliaLinked Comment: Vossen et al. Br J Dermatol 2017; 177:895-896.CorrespondenceJohn W. Frew.E-mail: Accepted for publication27 February 2017Funding sourcesNone.

Conflicts of interestNone declared.Hidradenitis suppurativa (HS) is a severe chronic inflammatory disorder characterizedby recurrent painful deep-seated nodules with a predilection to the apocrine-bearingareas of skin. A minority of cases of HS are due to mutations in the c-secretase com-plex. Contention exists surrounding the pathogenicity of sequence variants and theireffects upon Notch signalling. This systematic review was registered with PROSPERO(CRD42016041425) and was conducted in line with the PRISMA statement. Eligibil-ity criteria for this review included published case reports, case series and reviews thatidentified sequence variants or protein or functional studies from patients with HS.Sixty-two articles were identified reporting a total of 41 sequence variants - heterozy-gous missense (nine), splice site (nine), insertion resulting in frameshift (one), pre-mature termination codon (19) and promoter region PSTPIP1 (three) - with 18associated protein or functional studies. The American College of Medical Geneticsand Genomics standards and guidelines on the interpretation of sequence variantswere applied to each identified variant to assess evidence for pathogenicity. Twenty-three variants were assessed as likely pathogenic, 17 of uncertain significance and onebenign. The large number of variants of 'uncertain significance' is largely due to thevariable number of functional studies. Four studies used Notch as a proxy for c-secretasefunction, with conclusions of nonpathogenicity based on the assumption of Notchsignalling as the sole pathogenic process. The role of Notch-independent signallingmechanisms requires further research. Limitations to this study include identifica-tion of variants of Mendelian inheritance and not complex polygenic traits.

What's already known about this topic?*Hidradenitis suppurativa (HS) is a severe chronic inflammatory disorder, where aminority of cases are associated with sequence variants in the c-secretase complex.*The proposed pathogenesis for familial HS is downregulation of Notch signallingvia altered function of c-secretase.*Conflicting results exist regarding the pathogenicity of c-secretase variants in HS.What does this study add?*This systemic review has identified 41 distinct sequence variants in patients with HS.*Applying the American College of Medical Genetics and Genomics criteria, 23 vari-ants were likely pathogenic, 17 were of uncertain significance and one was benign.*The luminal sites of the majority of protein alterations in the c-secretase complex,along with lack of Notch downregulation, suggest the possibility of a Notch-inde-pendent pathway of pathogenesis in some cases of HS.Hidradenitis suppurativa (HS), also known as acne inversa, isa severe chronic inflammatory disorder characterized by recur-rent painful deep-seated nodules with a predilection to theapocrine-bearing areas of skin.1 A systematic review of knowngenetic sequence variants in HS has not previously beenundertaken.The underlying pathogenesis of HS relates to a dysregula-tion of the innate immune system resulting in an uncontrolledinflammatory response localized to the cutaneous follicularunit, resulting in destructive inflammation and the develop-ment of painful sinuses, abscesses and hypertrophic scarring.1It has strong associations with metabolic disease2 and otherinflammatory syndromes such as inflammatory bowel dis-ease,3,4 as well as an increased incidence in the setting of avariety of monogenic disorders5-7 such as Down syndrome.2

A strong autosomal dominant (AD) inheritance pattern isobserved in a minority of cases of HS,8,9 with a variable fam-ily history among the majority of individuals affected.8,9Since the initial identification of a four-generation Chinesekindred with HS to chromosome 1p21.1-1q25.3 by Gao et al.in 2006,8 and the subsequent identification of mutations inthe c-secretase complex by Wang et al. in 2010,10 over 26sequence variants have been reported as pathogenic variantsfor HS in the c-secretase complex in multiple kindred from avariety of ethnic backgrounds.11 The c-secretase complex iscomprised of nicastrin, presenilin 1, presenilin enhancer 2and anterior pharynx defective 1.The American College of Medical Genetics and Genomics(ACMG) has recently released guidelines for the stratificationof pathogenicity of sequence variants for Mendelian diseases.These guidelines define objective criteria for the stratificationof polymorphisms into five distinct categories: pathogenic,likely pathogenic, uncertain significance, likely benign andbenign. While the criteria are applicable only for Mendeliandisorders,12 the use of objective criteria to stratify identifiedsequence variants in HS will aid in establishing an objectivemeasure of pathogenicity. This may guide further investigationinto proteomic studies for certain variants, as well as thesearch for additional monogenic or polygenic 'risk alleles' inthe absence of a pathogenic variant.12

The importance of polygenic 'risk alleles' has becomeapparent as having direct relevance to disease severity andresponse to current therapeutic interventions, even in patientswith identified monogenic variants.13,14 Identification of theseadditional variants and the development of robust genotype-phenotype correlation may lead to explanations for the vari-able response to treatments in this disorder, as well as moreaccurate stratification in clinical trials and identification ofmore effective treatments for this highly burdensome disease.This systematic review aims to identify all published reportsof genetic polymorphisms in both familial and sporadic HS. Itwill also collate any protein or functional studies pertaining tothese identified variants. The identified sequence variants willbe critically evaluated using the ACMG standards and guideli-nes for the interpretation of sequence variants12 using prede-fined objective criteria. This will produce a classification ofsequence variants in HS to guide further functional studies,clarify the role of specific sequence variants in HS and provideinsights into the pathogenesis of the disease

MethodsThis systematic review was registered with PROSPERO15 (reg-istration number CRD42016041425) and was conducted inline with the PRISMA statement.16 Eligibility criteria for thisreview included published case reports, case series and reviewsthat (i) included patients with clinically diagnosed HS1 and(ii) included identified sequence variants from patients withHS or contained protein functional studies based on previouslyidentified sequence variants from patients with HS. No restric-tions on patient age, sex or ethnicity, or language restrictionswere applied.Information sources for this review encompassed MEDLINE(1946 to week 42 of 2016), Embase (1980 to week 42 of2016) and 'Epub ahead of print, and nonindexed citations', asshowninFigure 1.ThesearchstrategyislistedinAppendix S1 (see Supporting Information).Datacollectionwasperformedindependentlybytwoauthors, with any disagreements regarding inclusion of cita-tions being referred to a third author for mediation. Informa-tion was collected using a standardized data collection form(Appendix S2; see Supporting Information), with the principaloutcome of interest being identified sequence variants.Potential sources of bias in collating cases were acknowl-edged, including publications bias and reporting bias regard-ing the incidence of specific mutations and the rate of HSkindred with no identified mutation. Therefore no attemptswere made to quantify the most common sequence variants orthe proportion of kindreds without identified mutations dueto this bias being present. Results were collated using the stan-dardized data collection form. Two authors independentlyanalysed each sequence variant using the collated data availableagainst the ACMG guidelines (Table 1). Any disagreement wasreferred to a third independent author for mediation.ResultsStudy selection

The results of the search strategy are illustrated in Figure 1.Of the 533 citations screened, 135 articles were identified forfull-text retrieval and review. Of these 135 full-text articles,70 were excluded due to not pertaining to gene studies orfunctional analysis, with three articles being duplicate reportsof included cases either in abstract or review form. Sixty-twoarticles reported a total of 41 sequence variants, with 18sequence variants having associated protein functional studies.The identified sequence variants and their associated proteinand functional study data are summarized in Table 2. Forty-one individual sequence variants were identified encompassingthe NCSTN, PSEN1, PSENEN and PSTPIP1 genes, coding for nicas-trin, presenilin 1, presenilin enhancer 2 and proline-serine-threonine phosphatase-interacting protein 1, respectively.17-44The types of sequence variants encompassed heterozygousmissense mutations (nine variants),17-22,28,42-44 splice-sitemutations (nine variants),10,11,22-28,30 an insertion resultingin frameshift (one variant)11 and 19 variants resulting in pre-mature termination codons or protein truncation.10,11,20,26-36

Three variants29,32 involved increased numbers of CCTGrepeats the promoter region of PSTPIP1.Of the 41 discrete sequence variants, 18 had associated pro-tein or functional studies, including mRNA studies (eight vari-ants),10,23,26,30,32-35transcribedproteinstudies(fourvariants),10,20,26,30 functional studies of Notch activation (fourvariants)11,17,22,38 and in silico predictive algorithms (two vari-ants).25,32,33Twenty-sevenvariantswereassociatedwithfamilialHS,9-11,17,19-23,25,27-30,32-35,37sixwithsporadicHS10,18,29,30,33 and one (presumed nonsignificant) with bothfamilial and sporadic cases of HS (NCSTN c.1180-5C>G).22,27The inheritance of the remaining seven variants was notreported.24,26,29,31The individual sequence variants and protein domainsaffected in each gene are illustrated in Figure 2. Thirty variantsinvolved NCSTN,10,11,17-37 three involved PSEN110,22,30 andthree involved PSENEN.10,35 All identified sequence variants inthe NCSTN and PSENEN genes involved the luminal componentsof their respective proteins. Of the three variants in PSEN1,only one splice-site variant involved the cytoplasmic loopdomain of the presenilin 1 protein.22 Examining the distribu-tion of sequence variants in NCSTN, no predilection could beidentifiedpertainingtoanyspecificproteindomainor

component of the nicastrin protein. Four of the six identifiedmissense sequence variants were located adjacent to or withinthe Lid protein domain, which is proposed to be a vital bind-ing site for nicastrin.19-21,28 In fact, the only missense variantwith confirmed decreased levels of Notch intracellular domain(ICD) involves a missense mutation in exon 6, within the Lidproteindomain.28Furtherproteomicstudieswouldberequired to validate the hypothesis that disease-causing mis-sense mutations are localized to this vital substrate-bindingarea.A number of additional genes were reported to be identi-fied in the setting of HS but not associated with the disease(Table 3). These included case reports of epidermolysis bul-losasimplex,38Dowling-Degosdisease7andkeratitis-ichthyosis-deafness (KID) syndrome.5,6 Given the lack ofwhole-genome sequencing in these case reports it is unknownwhether any known sequence variants in the c-secretase com-plex are present in these cases. Incidental association of HSwith these conditions is more likely than a causative linkbetween these diseases, unless further evidence emerges to thecontrary. Evidence of cross-talk between Notch signalling andgap junction proteins does occur in embryonic develop-ment,39,40 but it is unclear whether the association of HS andKID syndrome is more than incidental.Reports of sequence variants that might augment the sever-ity and onset of HS and possibly treatment response were alsoidentified.13,14 Haplotypes of tumour necrosis factor-a andinterleukin-(IL)-12 augment the onset and severity of HS andmay be confounding alleles in the pathogenicity of c-secretasesequence variants.13,14 Reports of individuals with HS associ-ated with other complex polygenic disorders have identified anumber of sequence variants and haplotypes that associate(c) 2017 British Association of DermatologistsBritish Journal of Dermatology (2017) 177, pp987-998

with HS (Table 3).3-7,41-46 These variants would be appropri-ate for further exploration in a polygenic model of disease inindividuals with sporadic HS.American College of Medical Genetics and Genomicspathogenicity criteria analysisThe evaluation of each identified sequence variant against theACMG criteria12 is summarized in Table 1, with the criteriaexplained fully in Table S1 (see Supporting Information).Twenty-threevariantswereassessedaslikelypathogenic,11,17,18,20,23,28-36 17 were of uncertain signifi-cance11,19-22,24-26,28,31,35 and one was benign.22,27 The onebenign variant was classified as such due to the high rate ofthis variant in the European population (124%). Therefore itmay be a candidate for a polygenic risk allele in future studiesof sporadic HS. The classifications of sequence variants 1, 5, 8and10(NCSTNc.223G>A,c.477C>A,c.553G>Aandc.632C>G)17-19,25 were problematic due to assumptions madein the interpretation of Notch protein functional studies with-out direct evidence of protein or functional changes in the c-secretase complex. The addition of this BS3 criterion (Table 1)is questionable as it strongly contradicts the other evidencepertaining to sequence variants 1, 5 and 8 in these highly seg-regated kindred.Discussion

As well as being implicated in familial HS,8 c-secretase com-plex dysregulation is best known for its implication in early-onset and familial Alzheimer disease via Notch-independentmechanisms.47 To date no identified sequence variants havebeen described as being responsible for both Alzheimer dis-ease and HS,47 and no increased risk of Alzheimer disease hasbeen noted in patients or kindreds with HS.47c-Secretase dysregulation in HS has been suggested to resultin downregulation of the canonical Notch signalling path-way.48-50 This is proposed to be the 'major pathogenic mech-anism of HS'.49 Functional protein studies have producedvarying results regarding the impact of c-secretase sequencevariants on Notch signalling in HS,47,48 leading to contentionregarding the pathogenicity of variants without downregulatedNotch signalling.51,52 Disparate effects upon Notch signallinghave been observed in PSEN1 sequence variants, dependent onthe location of sequence variants and resultant truncationofthepresenilin1protein.47UpregulationofNotchsignalling was noted to occur as a result of PSEN1 c.725delC,47in direct contradiction to the existing proposed pathogenesisof HS.47-50Sequence variants have been identified in both sporadic andfamilial cases of HS,8,9 with no single significant variant hav-ing been reported in both familial and unrelated sporadiccases.8,9,11 This suggests variable levels of function of theresulting protein and heterogeneous impact upon Notch andNotch-independent signalling mechanisms. Well-documentedHS kindreds exist with no evidence of sequence variants in the

c-secretase complex,10,53 suggesting involvement of additionalloci in the pathogenesis of the disease. Unfortunately, func-tional studies of Notch signalling have not been conducted inall kindreds (with and without identified sequence variants) inorder to assess whether associated loci also function throughdownregulation of Notch signalling. No additional genes asidefrom those associated with monogenic autoinflammatory syn-dromes (PASH/PAPASH; see below)54-56 have been implicatedto date.The roles of environmental risk factors in the developmentof sporadic HS are well established2 and have been circum-stantially linked to dysregulation of Notch signalling.49,50 Allof the environmental risk factors that exacerbate or precipitateHS are proinflammatory in nature and do not exclusivelyimpact the Notch signalling pathway.2 From a clinical stand-point, obesity, smoking and insulin resistance are stronglyrelated to a subset of cases of sporadic HS,57 although somegenetic predisposition to the development of HS on a back-ground of these environmental triggers is likely at play.Circumstantially, environmental triggers are not as potent aspathogenic stimulators in familial HS,57 although they mayexplain one case report of lack of segregation of sequencevariants in a Japanese family.58 The high prevalence of theseenvironmental and metabolic triggers in the absence of HS inthegeneralpopulationimpliesadditionalpredisposingsequence variants outside of the Notch signalling pathway toaccount for their strong association with sporadic HS. Thepossibility of a polygenic form of inheritance comprisinghigh-frequency, low-penetrance 'risk alleles' in sporadic HShas not yet been explored.12

This review has identified 41 distinct sequence variantsreported in association with HS, encompassing three of thefour components of the c-secretase complex (nicastrin, prese-nilin 1 and presenilin enhancer 2), as well as variants in thepromoter region of PSTPIP1. Both HS and HS associated withPASH (pyoderma gangrenosum, acne and HS) and PAPASH(pyogenic arthritis, pyoderma gangrenosum, acne and HS)syndromes were identified as being caused by mutations in  Colour Coding: Red= Truncang; Green= Missense; Blue= Splice site; TM=Transmembrane Domain Nicastrin (NCSTN) Presenilin-1 (PSEN1) PEN2 (PSENEN)  (c) 2017 British Association of DermatologistsBritish Journal of Dermatology (2017) 177, pp987-998components of the c-secretase complex. This lends credenceto the concept of HS being an autoinflammatory disorder andon a spectrum with PASH and PAPASH. PSTPIP1 and Toll-likereceptor 4 are also cleaved by the c-secretase complex (orassociateddownstreammediatorssuchasADAM10andADAM17) and these may be the link between c-secretasesequence variants and the inflammasome in autoinflammatorydisorders.59The sites of the sequence variants in the luminal domainsof the resultant proteins are in contrast to the transmembranesite of Notch ICD cleavage, although steric alterations to thetransmembrane domains of these proteins cannot be con-firmed without further proteomic studies. The large numberof variants identified as being of 'uncertain significance' is duelargely to the variable number of functional studies that havebeen undertaken in the setting of the identified variants and

the conclusions of those studies. Identification of < 20% ofthe identified variants (eight of 41) involved protein or func-tional studies examining mRNA, or protein studies of compo-nents of the c-secretase complex (Table 2). An additional fourvariants (all missense variants)19-21,48 had accompanying pro-tein studies looking at Notch ICD as a proxy for c-secretasefunction. Only one missense variant in the Lid domain ofnicastrin (NCSTN c.647A>C) demonstrated impaired NotchICD signalling.51 The authors concluded that the variants withno impairment in Notch ICD signalling were nonpathogenic.51This conclusion is based on the premise that Notch ICD sig-nalling is the sole pathogenic process in HS.Our understanding of the workings of the Notch signallingpathway is still incomplete, and evidence from Alzheimer dis-ease research has demonstrated that sequence variants in com-ponents of the c-secretase complex have disparate effects onNoReferenceGene/sequencevariantFunction of geneAssociationComments141SULT1B1NRConjugation ofhormones,neurotransmitters,drugsAssociation in the settingof HS/AI with IBDRisk allele for HS inIBD241SULT1E1NROestrogen metabolismAssociation in the settingof HS/AI with IBDRisk allele for HS inIBD341ELOVL7NRRegulation of lipidmetabolism viaPPAR-aProtective association inthe setting of HS/AIwith IBDProtective allele forHS in IBD

AI, acne inversa; IBD, irritable bowel disease; KID, keratosis-ichthyosis-deafness syndrome; NR, not reported; PC, pachyonychia congenita;PPAR, peroxisome proliferator-activated receptor; SNP, single-nucleotide polymorphism.(c) 2017 British Association of DermatologistsBritish Journal of Dermatology (2017) 177, pp987-998cytosolic (Notch ICD) and luminal (extracellular) signalling,which vary according to the proteomic effect of the sequencevariant.47 Variants with no effect on Notch ICD signalling canstill exert a deleterious effect on luminal signalling and viceversa.47A plethora of proteins and receptor constructs are processedby the transmembrane and luminal portions of the c-secretasecomplex and associated 'sheddases'.59 This is achieved even inthe absence of nicastrin protein, as nicastrin functions bysteric inhibition of long extracellular domains of extracellularligands.60,61 Hence any abnormalities in nicastrin proteinstructure or function may result in dysregulation of transmem-brane receptor-ligand complex cleavage, as well as dysregula-tion of the Notch ICD.59-61 Such transmembrane receptorsinclude ErbB receptors [such as epidermal growth factorreceptor (EGFR) and human epidermal growth factor receptor2 (HER2)], IL-1bR1 and IL-1bR2, IL-6R and PSTPIP precur-sors,59 which have vital roles in inflammation.In the study by Xiao et al., complete absence of nicastrin (in-duced via small interfering RNA) does not demonstrate evi-dence of EGFR upregulation;48 however, the presence of anenzymatically inactive c-secretase complex (as measured byNotch/Hes-1 activity) does lead to upregulation of EGFR,62 amechanism independent of Notch signalling. Some degree ofhomeostatic regulation of this receptor upregulation can occur;however, this regulation is lost in the presence of a proinflam-matory trigger mediated by high levels of IL-1b and IL-6.63

Therefore, proinflammatory environmental triggers may havethe potential to trigger dysregulation of c-secretase-mediatedinflammatory responses via Notch-independent pathways.Additional evidence supporting this premise is present instudies of dysregulated cytokines in HS (such as IL-6, IL-1band IL-17),64 as well as reports of drug-induced HS65-67related to dysregulation of the ubiquitous tyrosine kinase-mediatedpathwaysPI3K-AKTandRas-Raf-MEK-ERK,theextracellular receptors of which undergo c-secretase-mediatedcleavage (including the EGFR and vascular endothelial growthfactor receptor families).59 In drug-induced HS due to mito-gen-activated protein kinase kinase (MEK) inhibitors,65,66

phosphoinositide 3-kinase (PI3K) activity is upregulated viarelease of cross-inhibition.68 The PI3K/AKT pathway has beenidentified as being upregulated in HS with pathogenic muta-tions in NCSTN.48 It is also incongruous that if Notch ICD sig-nalling is the sole pathogenic process in HS, there are noclinical reports of HS associated with other inherited diseasesinvolving intracellular Notch signalling68,69 [including cerebralautosomal dominant arteriopathy with subcortical infarcts andleukoencephalopathy(CADASIL)andAlagillesyndrome],whereas conditions with protein precursors cleaved by c-secretase (PASH and PAPASH)54-56 have strong associationswith HS.Therefore it is conceivable that a proportion of cases of HSmay be mediated via dysregulation of c-secretase complex-mediated cleavage of ligands other than Notch. This wouldimply that a lack of abnormal Notch ICD signalling (in thesetting of an otherwise likely pathogenic sequence variant inc-secretase) does not exclude the variant from being poten-tially pathogenic in HS.Evaluation of the identified sequence variants using the ACMGguidelines and recommendations is appropriate for Mendeliandisorders such as familial HS.12 Using these criteria in morecomplex non-Mendelian disorders can potentially lead to falselyidentifying contributing sequence variants as benign. Such'high-frequency, low-penetrance' alleles may exist in nonspo-radic HS, and the identification of such risk alleles12 is bestachieved using appropriately designed linkage and association.70

To this end we have noted in Table 2 whether the identifiedsequence variants are found in familial or sporadic cases of HSwhenever possible de novo mutations may present as a case ofsporadic HS. However, time and further family history mayreveal an AD inheritance pattern in subsequent generations.Further correlation is required to confirm the delineation offamilial variants from those present in sporadic cases, whichmay be amenable to further investigation for risk alleles. It isimportant to note that the boundary between familial and spo-radic disease is not fixed, as one Japanese kindred demon-strates,23 where the penetrance of a given sequence variantmay be altered by environmental factors such as smoking.13The benefit of investigating only cases and kindreds with spo-radic HS for additional 'risk alleles' is that the lack of ADinheritance implies a lower penetrance of disease alleles com-pared with AD kindreds. Hence, comparing affected and unaf-fectedindividualsismorelikelytoallowaccurateidentification of risk alleles that can be validated in other pop-ulations.In conclusion, this systematic review has identified 41 dis-tinct sequence variants in HS. Of these 41 variants, 23 areconsidered pathogenic and 17 likely pathogenic, with onlyone excluded as benign. Three variants18-20 were shown notto impact the intracellular Notch signalling pathway, bringinginto question whether a certain proportion of cases may actvia a Notch-independent pathway. Further proteomic andfunctional studies are required to correlate the pathogenicfunction of sequence variants in the c-secretase complex inHS. The concept of cases of sporadic HS being a complexpolygenic disorder has not been explored and is worthy offurther attention given the known association of disease-alter-ing variants in familial HS.
